summary: A VLS question ties present magazine depth to end-of-history procurement assumptions and the long dominance of the Burke design.
body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the Flight III's 96 cells with late-Cold-War concepts carrying roughly 144 to 160. A magazine that looked sufficient in a quieter era is harder to justify when fewer hulls must sustain longer combat. The same logic applies strongly to today's smaller Royal Navy, where each ship carries more of the fleet's burden.

evidence.note: The answer links current cell counts to post-Cold War assumptions and compares 96 cells with earlier 144-160-cell concepts.

question: Why do contemporary US Navy warship designs not carry more VLS cells?
answerShort: Many designs reflect post-Cold War assumptions, and even Burke Flight III retained 96 cells despite earlier replacement concepts considering much deeper magazines.

title: Can rising powers redefine maritime law?
videoTitle: Bruships 244: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A maritime-law answer explains the tension among custom, precedent, written rules, treaty ratification, and naval enforcement.
body: Dr. Clarke estimates that only a small portion of maritime law is formally codified, leaving historical precedent to carry much of the system. That creates space for states to establish competing modern precedents, but assembling agreement is difficult because every participant wants exceptions. The United States illustrates the complexity: it has not ratified UNCLOS, yet enforces many of its principles because open seas serve American interests.

evidence.note: The response distinguishes precedent, written custom, and codified law and uses US UNCLOS practice as an example.

question: Are powers such as China trying to redefine maritime law that developed under Western control of the oceans?
answerShort: Much maritime law rests on precedent rather than universally codified statute, so new behavior can reshape practice, while even the United States enforces rules without ratifying UNCLOS.

title: How wartime service consumes machinery life
videoTitle: Bruships 244: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: A maintenance answer explains why apparently serviceable warships could be mechanically exhausted after wartime use.
body: Published peacetime operating limits assume safe, maintainable use and time for regular upkeep. War removes many of those margins because immediate survival and maximum power take priority. The resulting wear helps explain why age in calendar years alone understates the condition of ships considered for postwar retention.

evidence.note: The answer says wartime operation discards peacetime safety limits and drives machinery as hard as necessary.

question: Did the wartime penalty on machinery life seal the postwar fate of some First and Second World War ships?
answerShort: Yes; wartime operations discard peacetime safety and maintenance margins, so machinery is driven far harder and can consume several times its normal service life.

title: Reforming long-term UK defence procurement
videoTitle: Bruships 244: Naval History Questions Answered Live...
summary: The closing answer connects procurement reform to parliamentary continuity, accounting rules, industrial structure, and public strategy.
body: Dr. Clarke suggests moving major decisions away from the short life of individual governments, perhaps through stronger Defence Select Committee control. He also calls for clearer accounting and recognition that some yards are not ordinary competitive businesses: the submarine yard has one effective customer and monopoly characteristics. Stable procurement also depends on the public and politicians understanding which interests Britain must defend before a crisis makes the gap visible.

evidence.note: The discussion covers parliamentary continuity, accounting, the submarine-yard market, and public understanding of national interests.

question: How should the United Kingdom change its defence procurement system?
answerShort: The panel argues for longer-term cross-party control, less creative accounting, honest treatment of monopoly suppliers, and clearer public understanding of British security interests.

title: Italy's Mediterranean and Adriatic destroyer problem
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The opening frames Italian destroyers as a response to geography: long coastlines, nearby rivals, fleet screening, attack missions, and limited port infrastructure.
body: Dr. Clarke starts with Italy's awkward prewar position in the Mediterranean and Adriatic, where France and Austria-Hungary were both close enough to threaten the coast quickly. The watch point explains why Italian destroyers had to defend the battle fleet, fight enemy torpedo craft, attack hostile fleets, and operate from ports that were not always ready for larger modern warships. That strategic setting explains why the later designs keep balancing size, range, speed, and survivability.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Italy's distrust of neighboring powers and the rapid attack threat against a long coastline.
The transcript lists the combined need for coastline defense, fleet screening, attacks on enemy fleets, and support from constrained ports.

title: Fulmine and Benedetto Brin's compact destroyer logic
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The Fulmine discussion shows how Benedetto Brin's naval-industrial legacy produced a useful but tightly constrained early Italian destroyer.
body: This section links Fulmine to Benedetto Brin, the engineer-politician whose naval programs helped build Italian industrial capacity. Fulmine's 300-ton size, 26-knot speed, long low-speed range, and mixed gun and torpedo armament show the early design compromise: the ship had to fit Italian support facilities while still carrying enough fuel and weapons for real operations. The result was useful, including in the Italo-Turkish War, but it exposed the recurring space tradeoffs inside Italian destroyer design.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Fulmine and connects the ship to Benedetto Brin's naval engineering and industrial influence.
The section describes Fulmine's displacement, speed, range, crew, weapons, Italo-Turkish War usefulness, and the space limits created by fuel, machinery, ammunition, and personnel.

title: Lampo and the cost of chasing speed
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The German-built Lampo class illustrates the harsh range penalty of high speed and the difficulty of matching Italian missions to borrowed destroyer designs.
body: Italy turned to German yards for the Lampo class, but the ships brought the familiar seaworthiness problems of late nineteenth-century torpedo craft. Dr. Clarke contrasts their theoretical 31-knot performance with realistic sea speeds and then underlines the endurance collapse from 2,000 nautical miles at 12 knots to 290 nautical miles at 26 knots. The example is useful for understanding why destroyer speed could not be treated separately from range, sea conditions, and the infrastructure the ship had to use.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Lampo class as German-built, lists its machinery and armament, and describes poor seaworthiness and lower practical sea speed.
The section explains the infrastructure problem and compares low-speed range with the severe high-speed range drop.

title: Nembo and Morin's operational theory
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The Nembo class sits between destroyer and torpedo-boat thinking, with Enrico Morin's operational ideas shaping ships built around speed, torpedoes, and later oil-fired improvements.
body: Dr. Clarke uses Enrico Morin to show how Italian destroyer development was tied to ideas about organization and employment, not just hull dimensions. The Nembo class drew on Thornycroft examples and carried heavy torpedo armament for small ships, with some variants looking very close to torpedo boats by later standards. Their oil-fired boiler changes and improved 76 mm gun and torpedo layouts show the Italian Navy adapting existing hulls as doctrine and technology moved.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Enrico Morin, the Nembo class, its displacement, armament, and torpedo-heavy character.
The lecture ties the class to Thornycroft design influence, oil-fired boiler improvements, range figures, and later weapon changes.

title: Mirabello, Soldati, and a more professional fleet
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: Carlo Mirabello's reforms connect the Soldati class to training fuel, radio telegraphy, Adriatic defenses, and a push to make destroyers visibly central to Italian naval power.
body: This watch point is about the institutional setting behind the Soldati class. Mirabello improved radio telegraphy, gunnery schools, exercises, Adriatic defenses, and, crucially, doubled the coal supply available for training, which gave the fleet more practical professional depth. The Soldati destroyers then appear as more capable 400-ton-plus ships with 76 mm guns, 17.7-inch torpedo tubes, reloads, and mines, while their soldier-based names signaled that these small combatants mattered to national defense.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Carlo Mirabello and describes his work on radio telegraphy, gunnery, exercises, coal supply, Adriatic torpedo-boat defenses, Brindisi, and Taranto.
The section outlines Soldati displacement, speed, armament, torpedoes, mines, class naming, and the symbolic importance of destroyers.

title: Indomito and Cattolica's heavier destroyer calculation
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The Indomito class marks a jump to larger, faster, harder-hitting destroyers as Pasquale Cattolica weighs what will improve the odds in combat.
body: Dr. Clarke presents Pasquale Cattolica as a mathematically minded naval leader whose Indomito class reflected a more calculated search for combat advantage. The ships grew sharply from the Soldati scale to roughly 600-770 metric tons, carried a 4.7-inch gun, four 3-inch guns, and 450 mm torpedo tubes, and often exceeded their expected speed. The later wartime changes also show the risk of adding fuel and weapons without preserving efficiency: more fuel did not automatically mean more useful endurance.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Cattolica and explains the Indomito class's larger displacement, heavier armament, torpedo fit, and mathematical approach to combat odds.
The lecture describes the class's speed, machinery, wartime mine rails, gun changes, increased fuel capacity, and the resulting efficiency penalty.

title: Why Italy built better destroyers instead of enough destroyers
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: A procurement discussion explains why Italian destroyers improved qualitatively while scarce funds and other priorities kept the force too small.
body: The lecture pauses to explain that Italian destroyer policy did not rest on one stable grand theory. Frequent ministerial turnover meant the most influential figures were the ones who held office long enough to impose direction, while funding priorities favored torpedo boats, submarines, the battle fleet, cruisers, and minelayers. Dr. Clarke places Italy in a qualitative naval race, improving designs even when it could not build the numbers that a future Adriatic war would demand.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the lack of a single Italian destroyer theory and the importance of ministers who stayed in office long enough to shape policy.
The section describes torpedo boats, submarines, the battle fleet, cruisers, and minelayers taking priority while Italy participated in a qualitative naval arms race.

title: Rosolino Pilo and the destroyer shortage Italy felt in war
videoTitle: Building for National Survival: Italian Destroyers prior to WWI
summary: The final prewar class shows Italian destroyer design reaching useful wartime form, but the conclusion stresses that eight new ships were not enough for Adriatic operations.
body: The closing design discussion treats the Rosolino Pilo class as improved Indomito-style destroyers, ordered just before the war and entering service during it. Their emphasis on speed, machinery, guns, torpedoes, and mines made them useful fighting ships, but the fleet needed many more of them. Dr. Clarke ends by tying Allied destroyer support in the Adriatic to the central force-structure problem: Italian designs were capable, fast, and well balanced, but the destroyer arm was understrength when war came.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the final prewar class, its relationship to Indomito, displacement, postwar classification, guns, torpedoes, mines, turbine, boilers, speed emphasis, and World War I entry.
The conclusion states that eight ships were not enough and that Italian destroyer numbers were inadequate for Adriatic and World War I needs.

title: The cruising role before cruisers
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Cruisers are defined first by independent scouting, commerce work, and peacetime presence rather than by a fixed weapons count.
body: Dr. Clarke begins with the missions historically described as going cruising: independent scouting, commerce protection, commerce interdiction, and presence. In the age of sail these jobs usually belonged to fourth-, fifth-, and sixth-rate ships rather than the fleet's most powerful vessels. The peacetime presence mission is especially important because an affordable, well-handled ship can support diplomacy and demonstrate national competence without deploying a battle fleet.

evidence.note: The lecture defines the four cruising missions and explains the diplomatic value of presence.

title: Why Ibuki was not a battlecruiser
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: The Japanese Ibuki-era ships belong among large armoured cruisers because they predate the battlecruiser concept.
body: Classification should respect the concepts available when a ship was designed. These vessels can be described as pre-dreadnought cruisers, but calling them battlecruisers retroactively implies a design category their builders did not possess.

evidence.note: The response rejects retroactive battlecruiser classification.

question: Should the Japanese Ibuki-era ships be classified as battlecruisers or large armoured cruisers?
answerShort: They were large armoured cruisers; they were built before the battlecruiser concept existed.

title: How the ironclad frigate created the cruiser
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Once ironclad frigates became battle-fleet ships, navies needed a smaller and cheaper vessel to resume their traditional cruising work.
body: HMS Warrior illustrates how the frigate grew into an armoured ship comparable to the battleships of its day. Such ships were too costly and important to deploy everywhere, so the cruiser emerged as a reduced frigate able to scout, protect or raid commerce, and show the flag. Its armament needed to deter lesser ships and threaten stronger ones without making it a capital ship.

evidence.note: The lecture traces the frigate's growth and the resulting need for a cheaper cruiser.

title: A mission-based modern cruiser definition
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: A modern cruiser needs endurance, worldwide reach, independent usefulness, general-purpose capability, and value as a task-group picket or command ship.
body: A 1982 definition supplies a historical starting point, but aircraft, helicopters, satellites, and nuclear submarines have altered scouting and pursuit. Dr. Clarke retains endurance, seakeeping, shadowing, and enough combat power for independent action, while adding worldwide mobility and task-group command or picket work. A helicopter is a particularly valuable general-purpose requirement because it extends surveillance and anti-submarine capability.

evidence.note: The lecture updates the historical definition for modern sensors, aircraft, and task groups.

title: Was Long Beach the last true U.S. cruiser?
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: There is a credible case that USS Long Beach was the last American ship to embody the cruiser type fully.
body: The answer is deliberately qualified because later American ships retained some similar functions on smaller hulls. The exchange highlights how designation, size, endurance, and mission can point in different directions.

evidence.note: The response accepts the classification as a defensible interpretation.

question: Was USS Long Beach the last true American cruiser?
answerShort: There is certainly an argument for it, even though later ships followed parts of the same pattern.

title: Iver Huitfeldt on the cruiser spectrum
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Denmark's Iver Huitfeldt class combines general-purpose weapons, sensors, helicopter support, and exceptional range in a cruiser-like presence ship.
body: The class carries layered air defence, anti-ship missiles, guns, torpedoes, and a helicopter while remaining the Danish Navy's principal global presence platform. Its roughly 9,000-nautical-mile range at 18 knots matters more to the cruiser argument than a simple VLS total. The StanFlex approach also makes headline construction prices difficult to compare because weapon modules belong to a separate shared pool.

evidence.note: The lecture reviews the class's weapons, sensors, range, and presence role.

title: FREMM national variants and cruiser work
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: French and Italian FREMM variants share a family origin but diverge in size, speed, endurance, crew, weapons, and national operating priorities.
body: The FREMM program demonstrates how a multinational design becomes several distinct ships. Both French and Italian versions are capable general-purpose vessels rather than fleet-leading capital ships, which fits the historical cruiser role. Italian gun arrangements and the larger general-purpose variant reinforce their suitability for independent presence and diplomacy.

evidence.note: The comparison gives French and Italian dimensions, endurance, crews, missiles, and gun fits.

title: Constellation's gun and presence problem
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Constellation is likely to perform cruiser duties, but its 57 mm gun may lack the practical and symbolic weight expected in coercive presence missions.
body: The American FREMM derivative is larger and carries useful missiles and sensors, yet Dr. Clarke questions the choice of a 57 mm main gun. Presence depends partly on visible capability: historical ships were carefully presented to look effective when entering foreign ports. Because U.S. commitments will force Constellation-class ships into independent cruiser work, gun arcs and diplomatic effect matter alongside missile performance.

evidence.note: The lecture connects the 57 mm gun, visible presence, and likely U.S. cruiser duties.

title: Type 054A as a true frigate baseline
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: China's Type 054A is a numerous, balanced escort whose local presence and ASW value distinguish it from a globally independent cruiser.
body: The Type 054A supplies the comparison's frigate baseline: moderate displacement, useful missiles and sonar, a helicopter, and construction in large numbers. Its role is primarily task-group escort and regional presence rather than distant independent action. The number of hulls complicates submarine operations and reflects China's parallel investment in naval infrastructure and maintenance capacity.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the Type 054A's size, weapons, sensors, numbers, and escort role.

title: What would Dr. Clarke's Royal Navy use?
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: His outline centers on the largest Type 26 hull, a Type 83 concept, and a complementary submarine program.
body: The response points to a family of existing design work rather than inventing an entirely new fleet during the exchange. A large Type 26-derived surface combatant would supply the repeatable hull, with Type 83 and submarine concepts filling higher-end requirements.

evidence.note: The answer identifies the principal hull and complementary programs.

question: What would a Royal Navy designed for Britain's needs look like?
answerShort: It would build around the largest Type 26 variant, a Type 83 design, and a supporting submarine program.

title: Fridtjof Nansen and small-navy cruiser duties
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Norway's Fridtjof Nansen class spreads modest quantities of many capabilities across ships that must cover both escort and national presence work.
body: The class carries air defence, anti-ship missiles, torpedoes, a helicopter, and diplomatic utility without possessing a large magazine in any category. For a small navy, those ships inevitably assume some cruiser functions because there are few other hulls available for distant or politically visible missions. The loss of Helge Ingstad also illustrates how a small fleet feels the removal of even one multipurpose ship.

evidence.note: The lecture inventories the class and notes Helge Ingstad's operational history and loss.

title: Why the Horizon partnership split
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Britain, France, and Italy could share technology but not one Horizon ship because their fleet sizes and concepts of operation differed.
body: Britain wanted numerous globally deployable air-defence ships, France primarily needed escorts for Charles de Gaulle, and Italy sought an additional capability within a different fleet plan. Those requirements shaped different sizes, systems, and procurement priorities. The case shows why common multinational equipment can succeed even when a single standardized hull cannot.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts British global operations with French carrier escort and Italian fleet requirements.

title: Gorshkov capability meets production limits
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Admiral Gorshkov-class ships have cruiser-like weapons and duties, but slow construction and engine supply constrain the fleet they can form.
body: The class combines long-range missiles, air defence, a large gun, torpedoes, and a helicopter on a comparatively short-legged hull. Russia needs these ships for cruiser work because many older surface combatants are aging, yet build times and the loss of Ukrainian gas-turbine supply undermine ambitious force plans. Proposed larger variants remain dependent on solving propulsion and industrial problems.

evidence.note: The lecture reviews construction dates, weapons growth, endurance, and turbine constraints.

title: Why European carriers still need logistics
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: European carrier power depends on auxiliaries and sustainment, not merely on owning flight decks.
body: France and Italy already operate carriers, so a European force would not begin from zero. The harder problem is sustaining distant operations. Britain's leverage comes partly from the Royal Fleet Auxiliary, whose scale gives British forces a mobility and endurance advantage that visible combat systems alone cannot provide.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts existing carriers with the scarcity of European auxiliary capacity.

question: Would a European navy have to be built around new carriers?
answerShort: Not entirely; carriers already exist, but useful power projection also requires a substantial auxiliary and logistics fleet.

title: F125 and the peacetime-frigate trap
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Germany's F125 supports long peacetime deployments but lacks the layered air defence expected of a war-fighting frigate.
body: The ship has a useful 127 mm gun, anti-ship missiles, RAM launchers, and facilities for sustained deployments. Its weakness is that RAM is a close-in anti-missile system rather than broad point or area defence. The design therefore suits a world of persistent low-threat presence better than one demanding a balanced escort in major war.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts F125 weapons and deployment features with its missing air-defence layer.

title: Should fleet tankers use civilian standards?
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Civilian-standard tankers may reduce cost, but proven naval designs and defensive fittings offer more confidence in a contested fleet role.
body: Dr. Clarke questions why Germany would choose civilian construction when cost-effective naval auxiliary designs already exist. Domestic construction may explain part of the decision, but ships supporting combat forces still need credible protection and survivability provisions.

evidence.note: The response compares civilian construction with naval fleet-oiler designs and calls for defences.

question: What are the risks of Germany procuring fleet tankers built to civilian standards?
answerShort: They may be economical, but existing naval designs are available and the new ships still need suitable defensive features.

title: India's frigates as an industrial program
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Indian frigate families combine foreign design inputs with domestic construction to expand both fleet capability and shipbuilding competence.
body: The discussion follows India's acquisition and development path through Russian-derived and indigenous frigates. These ships must cover broad general-purpose and presence duties because the navy cannot assign a separate specialist hull to every mission. Repeated batches and local construction matter as much as any single weapon fit because they build an enduring industrial base.

evidence.note: The lecture discusses Indian frigate classes, construction batches, and fleet requirements.

title: Type 26 as a global cruiser family
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: British, Australian, and Canadian Type 26 variants reveal how one global-combat hull changes when each navy assigns it a different share of fleet-wide duties.
body: The British version can rely on Type 45 and future Type 83 air-defence ships, while Australia and especially Canada need their variants to cover more roles. That pressure drives different weapons, sensors, and costs on the same design foundation. The original 'global combat ship' label is revealing: endurance and worldwide presence already place the family firmly on the cruiser spectrum.

evidence.note: The lecture ties the global combat mission to differing British and Canadian force structures.

title: Why modern warships do not regain heavy armour
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Armour sufficient to stop modern anti-ship missiles would impose extreme weight, so designers emphasize damage control and systems resilience instead.
body: The Falklands experience does not lead directly back to battleship protection. Modern missiles combine large warheads and high impact energy, making meaningful external armour prohibitively heavy for ordinary surface combatants. Splinter protection and internal survivability remain useful, but they solve a different problem.

evidence.note: The response explains the mismatch between missile effects and practical armour weight.

question: Why did navies not add much more armour after the Falklands War?
answerShort: Because practical cruiser or frigate armour cannot reliably stop modern anti-ship missiles without an unacceptable weight penalty.

title: Frigates and destroyers as cruiser variants
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Small fleets make nearly every major escort perform the endurance and presence work once associated with cruisers.
body: Modern labels often describe specialization: frigates lean toward anti-submarine work and destroyers toward air defence, while both still conduct independent deployments and presence missions. Because peace is more common than war, endurance, affordability, and diplomatic utility shape everyday fleet value. The lecture's conclusion is therefore functional rather than nominal: many contemporary escorts are specialized cruiser variants.

evidence.note: The conclusion links modern escort specialization to the enduring cruising and presence role.

title: Why a warship is not a naval main battle tank
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: Modern escorts resemble vulnerable weapons platforms more than main battle tanks; ships designed to absorb repeated heavy hits were battleships.
body: A direct anti-ship missile hit can range from mission-killing damage to total loss depending on impact location and follow-on effects. Ordinary escorts are not built to exchange such blows repeatedly. Recreating that expectation would mean building a battleship-sized protective system rather than merely adding modest armour to a frigate.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts missile vulnerability with the battleship's designed ability to absorb hits.

question: What is the naval equivalent of a main battle tank's firepower, protection, and staying power?
answerShort: There is no close modern escort equivalent; the ship type designed to take heavy hits and continue fighting was the battleship.

title: Why replacement warships keep growing
videoTitle: Patreon 57: Modern frigates, cruisers by another name?
summary: New combatants grow because power, cooling, sensors, weapons, aviation, survivability, and future margins accumulate on the hull.
body: Blue-water endurance matters, but it is only part of the growth. A successor expected to combine anti-submarine and air-defence functions inherits equipment and support demands from multiple older types. Designers also need space, electrical power, cooling, computing capacity, and upgrade margin for systems not yet fitted at launch.

evidence.note: The answer links growth to equipment and combining Type 45- and Type 26-like roles.

question: What causes replacement warships to increase in displacement?
answerShort: The accumulated weight and volume of wider missions, modern systems, endurance, and future-growth margins make larger hulls necessary.
